topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien beneath my fingertips. I traced the elegant script detailing my childhood, my career milestones, even the seemingly intimate details of my deepest fears. Each sentence was a perfectly crafted piece of a life I vaguely recognized, yet none of it resonated as truly mine. A coldness began to seep into my bones, a slow, creeping chill that had nothing to do with the drafty room. I reread the passage about my first heartbreak, the description so vivid it felt like someone had watched me relive it. My breath hitched, catching in my throat. I looked around the study, the familiar bookshelves suddenly seeming like silent, judgmental witnesses. I pushed the document away, the sound of the paper sliding against the mahogany desk echoing too loudly in the silence.

[story 2]

I sat bolt upright, the chair scraping harshly against the hardwood floor. The glossy booklet lay open on the antique writing desk, the typeface sharp and undeniably authoritative. My hands trembled as I flipped through the pages, my gaze snagging on a paragraph describing a decision I had made years ago, a choice I distinctly remembered making differently. A tightness coiled in my chest, constricting my ribs. I forced myself to breathe, counting the seconds between each inhale and exhale, a futile attempt to steady the internal tremor. When I finally managed to look up, the room seemed too bright, the shadows too deep. I wanted to crumple the paper, to destroy the evidence, but my fingers remained frozen above the crisp surface, paralyzed by a profound sense of disorientation.

[story 3]

It started as a misplaced note, tucked inside a book I hadn't touched in months. Then, a full manuscript emerged, meticulously detailing my entire existence. Reading it was like watching a stranger perform a flawless imitation of me. My jaw tightened until my teeth ached. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room, the rhythm of my footsteps uneven and sharp. Every detail—the preferred coffee order, the obscure book I loved in college, the precise phrasing of a casual remark—was present, documented, yet hollow. I stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, feeling a strange pressure building behind my eyes, a heavy weight that made it difficult to focus on anything else.
